does: This paper introduces TensorBench, a benchmark comprising 199 feature-addition and refactoring tasks on an open-source compiler-based tensor framework that extends PyTorch. The benchmark evaluates coding agents by having them implement patches to the repository, then grading success through automated test execution that combines pre-existing regression tests with agent-generated tests. Tasks span diverse compiler and runtime components including sparse tensor formats, optimization passes, IR transformations, scheduler modifications, and numerical operators. The authors evaluate seven coding agents across three frontier model families and one open-weight model, measuring pass rates under a criterion requiring both preservation of pre-existing behavior and satisfaction of new feature requirements.
connects: This work addresses a critical challenge in repository-level code generation benchmarks identified in prior work: the difficulty of creating tasks that are simultaneously challenging for frontier models and reliably evaluatable without expensive human review. TensorBench builds on earlier code-generation benchmarks like SWE-bench and CodeActAgent by leveraging an existing comprehensive test suite as ground truth, eliminating the need for human evaluation at scale. The benchmark extends lines of work on evaluating LLMs for software engineering tasks and compiler-level code generation, drawing on established practices in tensor framework development where test-driven evaluation is standard practice. It combines insights from benchmarking methodology with practical testing infrastructure from production ML systems.
enables: This benchmark enables systematic evaluation of coding agents on complex, real-world compiler and systems tasks requiring deep technical understanding. By providing reliable automated evaluation for compiler-level work, TensorBench facilitates research into improving agent performance on non-trivial repository-level tasks beyond typical application-level code. The benchmark could drive development of agents better equipped to handle multi-file changes, understand framework internals, and implement both dense and sparse numerical optimizations. Future work could extend this evaluation paradigm to other mature frameworks with rich test suites, establish patterns for which agent architectures excel at systems-level coding, and investigate how agents can better leverage domain-specific testing infrastructure for self-correction and validation.
